items.rationale: He views sum-check as the key technique to minimize prover commitment costs and total prover work — the real scalability bottleneck — superior to FFT/univariate-based systems (Plonk, Groth16, FRI).
items.age_note: Core thesis stated 2022-12-28 and continuously reaffirmed through 2023-12; current.

citations.quote: sum-check-based SNARKs remain the most promising for minimizing total prover work, and that good hardware implementations will follow
the scalability bottleneck for awhile now has been prover time. And the right technique to unlock this is sum-check

items.rationale: His own flagship work; a faster lookup argument (Lasso) and lookup-centric zkVM (Jolt) that he believes will reshape SNARK design toward simpler, more auditable systems with order-of-magnitude prover speedups.
items.age_note: Launched 2023-08-10; actively being built and defended through 2023-12; current.

citations.quote: Introducing Lasso and Jolt, two new works that I hope will change how SNARKs are designed and built.
the initial Lasso implementation is about 10x faster than Halo2's lookup argument, and we expect 30x-40x after optimization

items.rationale: Most recent enthusiasm: D&P's tuning of Ligero/Brakedown over small/binary fields, especially for committing small values, which he thinks reshapes the hashing-based SNARK landscape and unlocks fast SNARKs for standard hashes.
items.age_note: Most recent major view (2023-11-20); weighted heavily for recency.

citations.quote: work just unveiled by Ben Diamond and Jim Posen (D&P) that, in my view, reshapes the landscape for hashing-based SNARKs
this paves the way for much faster SNARKs for standard hash functions like Keccak

items.rationale: Excited about hashing-based commitments that work over any field without expensive FFTs; expects them to beat EC-based commitments whenever small fields make sense, once recursion overhead is addressed.
items.age_note: Stated 2023-08-31 and 2023-09-03; reinforced by D&P recursion improvements 2023-11-20.

citations.quote: I'm excited about this approach to polynomial commitments and hopeful we'll see further improvements soon.
Long-term, I expect ... Brakedown to be better than EC-based commitments whenever working over small fields makes sense.

items.rationale: Sees near-term, high-impact applications amenable to super-efficient circuit-free protocols, with matrix-multiplication of small integers as the bottleneck — a target for sum-check-based methods.
items.age_note: Stated once, 2022-12-31; never restated in later corpus — treat as aged.

citations.quote: One that I think we'll see soon is neural network training/inference, the bottleneck in which is often repeated matrix multiplication of small integers.

items.rationale: He considers known attacks on non-interactive FRI at 80 bits feasible today, framing under-parameterized FRI verifiers (esp. dYdX StarkEx) as a systemic risk to the rollup ecosystem securing hundreds of millions.
items.age_note: Stated forcefully 2023-03-09 and reaffirmed 2023-04-18; ~11 months old by 2024-03-31 and not restated since, but never reversed — stance persists.

citations.quote: Known attacks on FRI when run non-interactively at 80 bits of security are feasible.
The dYdX StarkEx verifier remains at 80 bits of security today.

items.rationale: Worried that deployed SNARK-friendly hashes ship with too few security bits, making it premature to relax security assumptions in composed/recursive SNARKs.
items.age_note: Stated 2022-12-31; not restated later — aged stance, never reversed.

citations.quote: while projects are deploying Poseidon with at most 95 bits of security, it's premature to relax on this

items.rationale: Cautious that heavy reliance on recursive composition brings overhead and imperfectly-understood security, particularly with new SNARK-friendly hashes and at large recursion depth.
items.age_note: Stated 2022-12-29/30; recursion still discussed positively as a tool in 2023-12, but the security caution itself was not restated.

citations.quote: recursion brings both overhead and imperfectly-understood security, especially when new SNARK-friendly hash functions are used
I remain cautious about security especially at large depth

items.rationale: Argues the true sequential, non-parallelizable bottleneck for blockchain-scale proving is deep recursion; breaking work into tiny pieces raises depth and hurts latency.

citations.quote: The real sequential bottleneck to be wary of is deep recursion. You can't parallelize that

items.rationale: Believes today's FRI-based zkVMs/rollups are mis-optimized (proof-size over prover-time) and require a complete reconfiguration — from IOPs and commitments to hashes and zkVM design — to achieve scalable provers.

citations.quote: To achieve scalable SNARK provers, a complete reconfiguration of today's deployments is needed

risk_regime.rationale: Thaler is a cryptography researcher; across 18 months of statements he expresses no market, macro, or risk-appetite view, and his every post carries an explicit 'none of this is investment advice' disclaimer. His 'risk' language is exclusively about cryptographic security parameters, not portfolio risk.

notes: This corpus is almost entirely technical SNARK/proof-systems discourse, not asset commentary. Thaler names no tokens and takes no market posture, so tokens_excited/concerned and a directional risk_regime are empty/no_view by design rather than omission. His 'sectors' are cryptographic architectures. Recency weighting: most recent strong signals (2023-11/12) are the Diamond-Posen small-binary-field/Ligero-Brakedown direction and continued Lasso/Jolt build-out — these are weighted up. The FRI 80-bit security alarm (a tactical, named-target concern about dYdX StarkEx) was stated emphatically in 2023-03/04 and never restated nor reversed by 2024-03-31; reported as current stance with an age caveat. Note an evolution, not a reversal, on recursion: he stays security-cautious about deep recursion (2022) yet actively uses GKR/recursion as a tool in Jolt (2023-12) — cautious-but-pragmatic. No statements conflict in a way requiring a reversal record beyond the age notes above.
